title: Urban inequality, the housing crisis and deteriorating water access in US cities

data_scope.spatial_coverage: United States (and Puerto Rico); 50 largest US Metropolitan Statistical Areas; Portland (Oregon) case study
data_scope.headline_numbers: >500,000 US households (~1.1 million people) without running water in 2021; >71% urban; plumbing poverty rose in 12 of 15 largest cities (2013-2017 vs 2017-2021); people of color a majority without water in 12 of 15 largest cities (LA 82%, Miami 79%, SF 74%, Houston 71%); Portland +~56% since 2000
data_scope.custom_dataset: comparable metro definitions for 50 largest US MSAs, 1980-2020 (figshare 10.25422/azu.data.25724286)

core_conclusion: Household running-water access in US cities is deteriorating, especially since the 2008 financial crash. Plumbing poverty has urbanized (>71% urban in 2021), rose in 12 of the 15 largest cities in the latest period, and disproportionately affects households of color. A 'reproductive squeeze' of unaffordable housing, stagnant wages and cost-of-living pressure is pushing low-income, racialized households into homes without running water, exemplified by Portland, Oregon.
why_it_matters: Reframes lack of running water as an urban, high-income-country problem; links the post-2008 housing shock directly to water access via the 'reproductive squeeze'; quantifies a racialized burden across major cities; and casts doubt on US progress toward SDG 6 and SDG 11.
why_nature: Longitudinal, 50-city system-scale evidence; couples space, society and infrastructure (water, race, social reproduction); strongly policy-translatable (housing, utility shut-offs, Census reform); introduces transferable concepts ('reproductive squeeze', 'urbanization of water poverty').
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable-ML/SHAP attribution of the 'reproductive squeeze' -> template for attributing health-access deterioration to socioeconomic shocks
Reframe water security as a 2SFCA/R2SFCA service-area problem -> portability test of my healthcare accessibility methods
Multi-source data fusion (utility shut-offs, evictions, mobile phone, remote sensing) -> nowcast hidden waterless/unmet-demand populations
Coupled housing-water agent-based model -> space-human coupling for job-housing and facility-vs-price work
Longitudinal MGWR disentangling race vs income -> directly reusable in healthcare and mental-health accessibility studies
